title.emphasis: Scenario 2: The Employee Argument ("I am and have been this entire time an Employee")

section.para: This is the more fundamental challenge. If the totality of the relationship—the continuous provision of services (UGC, feedback, QA, AI training), the degree of control exerted by Google (via rules, algorithms, and gamification), and the integral nature of the work—establishes an employment relationship, the VRP structure becomes secondary.
If you are legally deemed a misclassified employee, the analysis shifts entirely from contract law to employment law.
Yes, if an employment relationship exists, Google assumes all the "burdens" of an employer. This fundamentally changes how compensation and IP are treated:

listitem.para: Wages vs. Prizes: If you are an employee, the work you perform is labor. You are entitled to wages (including minimum wage and overtime) for all hours worked, regardless of whether a specific task was successful. The VRP structure, which only pays for results, is generally incompatible with standard employment wage requirements. The "reward" is recharacterized as a wage, commission, or bonus. Failure to pay wages for labor performed constitutes wage theft.
Benefits and Protections: Employers are responsible for payroll taxes, workers' compensation, unemployment insurance, and potentially other benefits mandated by local labor laws.

IP Ownership (Work Made for Hire): In an employment context, intellectual property created by an employee within the scope of their employment automatically belongs to the employer. You would not "own" the vulnerability discovery to sell it back to Google via the VRP; Google owns it the moment you discover it.

title.emphasis: The Implications of Performing the Act but Not Reporting It

section.para: If you find a vulnerability (perform the act) but do not report it, and you are deemed an employee:

listitem.para: Compensation: You are still entitled to wages for the time spent performing the skilled labor required to find it.

Employee Duties: However, employees have a duty of loyalty to their employer. If your implied job function includes improving product security or quality, discovering a vulnerability (which is the employer's IP) and intentionally failing to report it would likely constitute a serious breach of your duties as an employee.
